transcript: For as long as I can remember, I've loved the murder mystery genre, and this obsession began when I bought my first book, Murder on the Orient Express. It has a simple story structure, a beautiful setting, and of course, the now infamous twist ending that got me absolutely hooked. So, as you can imagine, whenever there's a new screen adaptation of this story, I am both really, really excited and also absolutely terrified. This was only amplified when I found out that Kenneth Branagh was both directing and starring in a 2017 version. Now, with the next film, Death on the Nile, only a week away from release, I think it's finally time for me to explain why I believe this was a bad book adaptation. As I've already said, this film was directed by and stars Kenneth Branagh. Now, if you don't know who Kenneth Branagh is, he kind of formed his own little niche for himself of directing and starring in Shakespeare films. Let the doors be locked! I've always been in two minds about this. On one hand, it always seemed a bit egotistical to me, like no one else wanted to hire him, so he just thought he'd do it himself. I'm gonna go direct my own movie, with me playing the main character. On the other hand, I really respect that he gets to play the most interesting, complex, and of course famous characters that there are. I imagine this is what motivated him to take on the character of Poirot. Agatha Christie, she's after the Bible in Shakespeare. She's the most read author on the entire planet. That's pretty impressive. But unfortunately, unlike with Shakespeare, I just don't think he understands the murder mystery genre. And he just really doesn't understand the character of Poirot. Agatha Christie, I think, has real relish in storytelling. Murder on the Orient Express is a relatively short book without much of a way of extraneous detail. This makes it perfect for adaptation as a screenwriter gets to take lots of creative liberties within the story. Among the countless adaptations there was a version in 2010 starring David Suchet who played the character of Poirot over two decades. Now, this version to me is the perfect example of how to do this story. Yes, it is incredibly dark and moody and frankly depressing, but when you know the context of the story, that is entirely appropriate. It takes the story to a very dark place, I think. It makes the story compelling, engaging, and altogether it makes it a brilliant adaptation. They also allow David Suchet to explore the moral quandaries that Poirot is experiencing in this case, something that Branagh wants to deal with but really struggles to, I think partly due to the fact that he chose to make this film a comedy. Now, if you're watching this video, I'm going to presume it means that you already know the ending of the story, because you've either read the book or watched one of the adaptations. But I'm going to quickly run through the story in case you haven't seen it in a while or want a refresher. The story begins with Poirot having finished a case in a foreign country. He's waiting at a train station, about to go on holiday for a few days. However, once he gets to his next destination he is informed that he has to return back to England, so he wants to travel that night on the Orient Express. It turns out that the train is fully booked, however luckily for Poirot he knows the conductor of the line and they managed to get him on board. Whilst on the train he meets multiple characters including a man called Ratchet. Now Ratchetatchet is very shady and asks Poirot to be his bodyguard, because he fears for his life. Poirot declines, and during that night, Ratchet is killed. Also during the night, the train is hit by a snowstorm, meaning that all the passengers are stranded, and the killer could not get off the train. Poirot quickly discovers that Ratchet was actually a kidnapper called Cassetti, who kidnapped and killed a young child called Daisy Armstrong a few years earlier. As his interviews continue, he discovers that everyone on the train actually has a link to the Armstrong family, whether being employed by them or being a personal friend. This then eventually leads him to conclude that everyone on the train took it upon themselves to murder Cassetti as 12 members of the jury finally taking what they saw as just revenge on Cassetti. Now in the book this is where the story pretty much just ends. We don't really find out what Poirot does with this information, it just stops there. However most adaptations decide to carry on the story in order to show us Poirot dealing with this information and deciding what he's going to do with it. Okay, now that we've recapped the story, I think it's finally time to get into the film and why I believe it is not a good book adaptation. Now, although I'm talking about Kenneth Branagh's film, I do want to make some references back to the 2010 adaptation. most notably how the two films begin and what that tells us about the different attitudes that the directors had. In David Suchet's version, we begin with a close-up on someone's eyes as Poirot berates him for allowing someone to die. This man then kills himself in front of Poirot, and yet Poirot still doesn't back down or think he did anything wrong. A man like your friend Lieutenant always has choice and it was his choice to lie that brought him into difficulty with the law. This shows us a few things. One, that Poirot views everything as black and white. You are either guilty or not guilty and even even though this man didn't murder someone, his negligence did lead to a death and therefore he should be punished. It also displays Poirot's hatred for someone lying to him and how furious that makes him. This comes up a lot at the end of the film. But most importantly, we see that the focus is on characters. These close-up shots happen throughout the film and really allow us to get into the heads of what people are thinking and feeling at any point. Branagh on the other hand begins the film with a sweeping shot of Jerusalem. This demonstrates not only the film's higher budget but also the focus on aesthetics. Throughout the film there is beautiful scenery, large crowds and of course very famous people. This of course makes sense for a Hollywood blockbuster and is part of the reason why it was successful, due to the names tied to it. However without this focus on characters that Suchet's version has, everything just ends up feeling a little bit shallow. This opening scene is technically not in the book, the book only gives very broad strokes about the incident that Poirot was sorting out. This means that a screenwriter gets to take whatever creative liberties they like. But what is most important from this opening scene is that we know who Poirot is and what he stands for. However, unlike in the 2010 version, this isn't really what Branagh does. What we do see is Branagh seriously leaning in to Poirot's character trait of OCD-like tendencies. And these lead to his view of right and wrong, things being in balance. I can only see the world as it should be, and when it is not the imperfection stands out like the nose in the middle of a face. It makes most of life unbearable but it is useful in the detection of crime now of course this is one of Poirot's classic character traits but it does mean that the decision he makes at the end of the story holds little weight when really all it comes down to is him wanting things to be in balance because that's how he likes it rather than the 2010 version where Poirot is dealing with his own religious beliefs and how that type Thank you. to is him wanting things to be in balance because that's how he likes it, rather than the 2010 version where Poirot is dealing with his own religious beliefs and how that ties to his moral decisions. This is particularly frustrating because Branagh does make a big point about wanting to focus on this moral dilemma that Poirot faces. It carries a much more grey area of moral complexity. And it just could have been set up so much better in this initial scene. Instead, all we really learn about Poirot is that he likes his eggs the same size, has a ridiculous moustache, and doesn't need his cane for walking because he's obese and elderly, but actually just uses it as a weapon in fight scenes that you'll see more of throughout the film. As the film progresses, we begin to meet our cast of characters. For the most part, I'm okay with how these are portrayed. However, there are one or two who are just so against the text that I find it almost obnoxious. One of these characters is the first person we meet, Mary Debenham. God, I am so sorry, Daisy, but I absolutely can't stand how this is played. Mary Debenham is meant to be this really tough character in the book who brought everyone together to commit this crime. However, here she's just sickly sweet. I know your moustache. From the papers. Everything she says is just to make you love her as an audience member because at the end they try to trick you into thinking she may have committed this crime alone however they do a really bad job of making me like her and instead i just find her very annoying during the scene we also meet colonel slash doctor arbuthnot now this character is a combination of two from the book and it makes complete sense for them to do this. They did it as well in the 2010 version. As in the book, the character of the Doctor is really just there as a sounding board for Poirot, and isn't particularly useful to the plot. Therefore, by combining him with another character, he can become one of the 12 conspirators. We then meet Book, who is in charge of the Orient Express. Now, in this version, this character is an absolute playboy. This is a prostitute. She is. I am. Which he certainly isn't in the book. But there's no real reason why he shouldn't be. I mean, this film is meant to be a comedy. And honestly, I feel like he goes for a certain level of character development that no other character displays throughout the film, and I do respect that, despite him being slightly annoying. We see multiple people getting onto the train, and then we have this stupid moment where the Count jump-kicks a guy in his face just for taking his picture, in order to show us that in this version the Count is a dancer with serious anger issues. Now, the book the Count is indeed protective of his wife because she's hiding a massive secret. However he is not this insanely aggressive animalistic character as they display here. I do think it takes away from what I always viewed as a relatively sweet relationship between them. We then see Poirot getting onto the train and Branagh decides to use this as a chance to show his directing abilities by creating a long shot that goes throughout the train station and then along the coach as he moves to his room. Now this must have been incredibly difficult to film with multiple moving parts, extras, all the actors and characters and I do think they did a decent job but Branagh does say that he basically let the characters improv their parts in this moment in order to get a natural response, which normally I would think is a great idea. However, I don't think the actors really understood the fact that Poirot is not meant to be on this train. They booked an extra compartment just to make sure they were completely alone for this murder. Now seeing someone else get on the train, they should be panicked, freaking out. As they are in the 2010 version. Has anyone seen who's trying to get on the train? Secretly trying to communicate with each other what's happening. Oh no. This is what has to happen, Sue. This is what is happening. And as an audience member, if I didn't know the twist, there would be no indication or clues here that I could potentially pick up on and I've always found that a bit unfair in a murder mystery as there's no chance of me figuring things out alongside the detective because the characters aren't acting naturally and I think this does come down to them not having clear enough direction. Once on the train they settle down for a meal and then we meet the most egregious character adaptation there is. I would prefer in the future to be sent not with that man. Like should be seated with like. Oh my god, why is he a Nazi? Why on earth would they make him a racist Nazi? Just so you know, You're neither an Austrian, nor a German, nor a professor. This character is actually an American private eye. In the book, he goes undercover as this boisterous American carpet salesman. But for some reason here, they decide that he should go undercover as a racist Nazi? I just, I don't understand it. He later on later on apologized about being racist i'm sorry about the colored folks cracks but like why would this character choose to be anyway why could he not just be german or why could he not be american it makes absolutely no sense and the only reason it's in the script is so that mary can do this to mix your red wine and the white would be to ruin them both i like a good rose again this is there to make you like her more but god do i find it annoying we then then properly meet Ratchet as he tries to ask Poirot to become his bodyguard. Now Ratchet is meant to be creepy and old and super unnerving and I guess Johnny Depp pulls that off? But he doesn't even slightly meet the character description that Christie put down. He's meant to have a relatively bold domed head and false teeth this is who the 2010 version cast what's your name why are you asking well kelly is three days away on trip god that is so creepy and he fits the character description perfectly however of course branner was focused on getting the most famous people possible. Anyway, he asked Poirot to be his bodyguard and Poirot says no because I do not like your face which is a brilliant line from the book Poirot tries to tuck into bed but unfortunately during the night there are lots of knocking on doors and bells going off that make him wake up and stick his head out now I would go over the timeline in detail but honestly I feel like you should get this from the book and also Branagh just doesn't really talk about them much and he certainly doesn't use them to figure out people's alibis. The only thing that you really need to remember is that at some point he sees a woman in a red kimono. Anyway the next morning everyone is complaining because they're not going to make their connections and get where they need to go and then we finally get to the discovery of the body and I absolutely hate how this is filmed. They decide to do this god awful overhead shot which just looks terrible to me and they also make it a long take even though there's nothing impressive about that due to the fact that we can't even see their mouths move. He was stabbed. Long straight edge knife, multiple stab wounds, some shallow, some cut clean through muscle. Can you estimate the time of death? It's hard to say with the window open but my best guess is is between midnight and two. Book wants to convince Poirot to take the case by saying this. You sit in a chair and you eat your cake and you think until the solution presents itself. You think that is what I do, I sit in a chair and I have a little piece of cake and then I come up with a great idea. Well I don't know what you do. If you know Poirot, you know that how he worked is by sitting back, thinking, using his little grey cells, eating some cake and using the psychology to solve the case. With this line Branagh is clearly saying that's not who he's playing because he wants to make Poirot an action star and I just don't understand why he'd choose to play the character of Poirot when he clearly doesn't like the character of Poirot because he doesn't like his introspective nature. Poirot is at the end of his rope, having been lied to and manipulated the entire time he's been on the train, and he is furious. No, it is medieval! The rule of law, it must be held high. And if it falls, you pick it up and hold it even higher. For all society, all civilized people will have nothing to shelter them if it is destroyed. There is a higher justice than the rule of law, monsieur. Then you let God administrate, not you! You can't tell these people are good people. That Cassidy... That Cassidy, he deserved to be executed for what he did and no one knows it was a prophecy that he was not! Lock the door. However, when it comes to telling the police, he actually decides to go with their version of events, that someone snuck onto the train in a conductor's outfit and then escaped through the window when the train was stopped. Instead of him then having a cheesy speech to them about how they need to move on with their lives after this event, we see him silently look back at them and then walk away through the snow whilst holding back tears and clutching his rosary, begging for forgiveness as he's still not sure if what he did was morally right or not. It's an incredibly powerful and emotional ending. And this is why Branagh's ending was so disappointing as there was no moral quandary, there was no emotion which he claimed he really wanted to focus on. And I think that was just lost for the sake of comedy and a feel-good aspect of a film. Or at the very least, their ending was in no way as impactful as the version done seven years before. But unfortunately, My name is Hercule Poirot and I am probably the greatest detective in the world. But this character is simply not Poirot. He's an action star who doesn't think about anything and just does not act in the way that Agatha Christie wrote him. timestamps.text: For as long as I can remember, I've loved the murder mystery genre, and this obsession began when I bought my first book, Murder on the Orient Express. It has a simple story structure, a beautiful setting, and of course, the now infamous twist ending that got me absolutely hooked. So, as you can imagine, whenever there's a new screen adaptation of this story, I am both really, really excited and also absolutely
terrified. This was only amplified when I found out that Kenneth Branagh was both directing and
starring in a 2017 version. Now, with the next film, Death on the Nile, only a week away from
release, I think it's finally time for me to explain why I believe this was a bad book adaptation.

As I've already said, this film was directed by and stars Kenneth Branagh. Now, if you don't know who Kenneth Branagh is, he kind of formed his own little niche for himself of directing and starring in Shakespeare films. Let the doors be locked! I've always been in two minds about this. On one hand, it always seemed a bit egotistical to me, like no one else wanted to hire him, so he just thought he'd do it himself. I'm gonna go direct my own movie, with me playing the main character. On the other hand, I really respect that he gets to play the most interesting, complex, and of course famous characters that there are. I imagine this is what motivated him to take on the character of Poirot. Agatha Christie, she's after the Bible in Shakespeare. She's the most read author on the entire planet. That's pretty impressive. But unfortunately, unlike with Shakespeare, I just don't think he understands the murder mystery genre. And he just really doesn't understand the character of Poirot. Agatha Christie, I think, has real relish in storytelling. Murder on the Orient Express is a relatively short book without much of a way of extraneous detail. This makes it perfect for adaptation as a screenwriter gets to take lots of creative liberties within the story. Among the countless adaptations there was a version in 2010 starring David Suchet who played the character of Poirot over two decades. Now, this version to me is the perfect example of how to do this story. Yes, it is incredibly dark and moody and frankly depressing, but when you know the context of the story, that is entirely appropriate. It takes the story to a very dark place, I think. It makes the story compelling, engaging, and altogether it makes it a brilliant adaptation. They also allow David Suchet to explore the moral quandaries that Poirot is experiencing in this case, something that Branagh wants to deal with but really struggles to, I think partly due to the fact that he chose to make this film a comedy. Now, if you're watching this video, I'm going to presume it means that you already know the ending of the story, because you've either read the book or watched one of the adaptations. But I'm going to quickly run through the story in case you haven't seen it in a while or want a refresher. The story begins with Poirot having finished a case in a foreign country. He's waiting at a train station, about to go on holiday for a few days. However, once he gets to his next destination he is informed that he has to return back to England, so he wants to travel that night on the Orient Express. It turns out that the train is fully booked, however luckily for Poirot he knows the conductor of the line and they managed to get him on board. Whilst on the train he meets multiple characters including a man called Ratchet. Now Ratchetatchet is very shady and asks Poirot to be his bodyguard, because he fears for his life. Poirot declines, and during that night, Ratchet is killed. Also during the night, the train is hit by a snowstorm, meaning that all the passengers are stranded, and the killer could not get off the train. Poirot quickly discovers that Ratchet was actually a kidnapper called Cassetti, who kidnapped and killed a young child called Daisy Armstrong a few years earlier. As his interviews continue, he discovers that everyone on the train actually has a link to the Armstrong family, whether being employed by them or being a personal friend. This then eventually leads him to conclude that everyone on the train took it upon themselves to murder Cassetti as 12 members of the jury finally taking what they saw as just revenge on Cassetti. Now in the book this is where the story pretty much just ends. We don't really find out what Poirot does with this information, it just stops there. However most adaptations decide to carry on the story in order to show us Poirot dealing with this information and deciding what he's going to do with it. Okay, now that we've recapped the story, I think it's finally time to get into the film and why I believe it is not a good book adaptation. Now, although I'm talking about Kenneth Branagh's film, I do want to make some references back to the 2010 adaptation. most notably how the two films begin and what that tells us about the different attitudes that the directors had. In David Suchet's version, we begin with a close-up
on someone's eyes as Poirot berates him for allowing someone to die. This man then kills

himself in front of Poirot, and yet Poirot still doesn't back down or think he did anything wrong. A man like your friend Lieutenant always has choice and it was his choice to lie that brought him into difficulty with the law. This shows us a few things. One, that Poirot views everything as black and white. You are either guilty or not guilty and even even though this man didn't murder someone, his negligence did lead to a death and therefore he should be punished. It also displays Poirot's hatred for someone lying to him and how furious that makes him. This comes up a lot at the end of the film. But most importantly, we see that the focus is on characters. These close-up shots happen throughout the film and really allow us to get into the heads of what people are thinking and feeling at any point. Branagh on the other hand begins the film with a sweeping shot of Jerusalem. This demonstrates not only the film's higher budget but also the focus on aesthetics. Throughout the film there is beautiful scenery, large crowds and of course very famous people. This of course makes sense for a Hollywood blockbuster and is part of the reason why it was successful, due to the names tied to it. However without this focus on characters that Suchet's version has, everything just ends up feeling a little bit shallow. This opening scene is technically not in the book, the book only gives very broad strokes about the incident that Poirot was sorting out. This means that a screenwriter gets to take whatever creative liberties they like. But what is most important from this opening scene is that we know who Poirot is and what he stands for. However, unlike in the 2010 version, this isn't really what Branagh does. What we do see is Branagh seriously leaning in to Poirot's character trait of OCD-like tendencies. And these lead to his view of right and wrong, things being in balance. I can only see the world as it should be, and when it is not the imperfection stands out like the nose in the middle of a face. It makes most of life unbearable but it is
useful in the detection of crime now of course this is one of Poirot's classic
character traits but it does mean that the decision he makes at the end of the
story holds little weight when really all it comes down to is him wanting
things to be in balance because that's how he likes it rather than the 2010
version where Poirot is dealing with his own religious beliefs and how that type

version where Poirot is dealing with his own religious beliefs and how that ties to his moral
decisions. This is particularly frustrating because Branagh does make a big point about
wanting to focus on this moral dilemma that Poirot faces. It carries a much more grey area of

moral complexity. And it just could have been set up so much better in this initial scene. One point is that the events that happen during the night are all done for Poirot's benefits so they want him to wake up and stick his head out and see the things they're trying to show him in order to mislead him. However, when it comes to telling the police, he actually decides to go with their version of events, that someone snuck onto the train in a conductor's outfit and then escaped through the window when the train was stopped. Instead of him then having a cheesy speech to them about how they need to

move on with their lives after this event, we see him silently look back at them and then walk away through the snow whilst holding back tears and clutching his rosary, begging for forgiveness as he's still not sure if what he did was morally right or not. It's an incredibly powerful and emotional ending. And this is why Branagh's

Thank you for me I could forgive the weird characters the changes for the sake of Hollywoodizing it but I can't forgive them not giving the information to the audience to figure it out just for the sake of the twist no no no no no it doesn't work the idea of the mystery is that we can figure it out, is that the characters act natural, is that the information that Poirot has is also given to us and set down for us so that we can work it out. And I just don't think that's done well here. I also truly feel that Branagh was not playing the character of Poirot. If I viewed him as another detective, I actually didn't mind this film too much. But unfortunately, My name is Hercule Poirot and I am probably the greatest detective in the world. But this character is simply not Poirot. He's an action star who doesn't think about anything and just does not act in the way that Agatha Christie wrote him. All I can say is but I really hope that Branagh did some further research on the character of Poirot and how to write an engaging murder mystery story rather than just an action film before he worked on Death on the Nile.
